title: Calculate conditional probability
description: Practice calculating conditional probability, that is, the probability that one event occurs given that another event has also occurred.

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers surveyed one hundred students on which superpower they would most like to have. The two-way table below displays data for the  sample of students who responded to the survey.

Superpower | Male | Female | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: |
Fly | $30$ | ${10}$ | $40$
Invisibility | $12$ | ${32}$ | $44$
Other | $10$ | ${6}$ | $16$
**TOTAL** | $52$ | $48$ | $100$

**Find the probability that the student was male, given the student chose to fly as their superpower.**

 $P\left( \text{male }| \text{ fly} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the student chose to fly as their superpower. There were $\purpleD{40}$ total students in the sample who chose to fly, and $\greenD{30}$ of them were male.

Superpower | Male | Female | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: |
Fly | $\greenD{30}$ | ${10}$ | $\purpleD{40}$
Invisibility | ${12}$ | ${32}$ | $44$
Other | ${10}$ | ${6}$ | $16$
**TOTAL** | ${52}$ | ${48}$ | $100$

$P\left( \text{male }| \text{ fly} \right)=\dfrac{\greenD{30}}{\purpleD{40}}=\dfrac{3}{4}=0.75$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}
P\left( \text{male }| \text{ fly} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{male and fly} \right)}{P\left( \text{ fly} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{{\dfrac{\greenD{30}}{100}}}{\dfrac{\purpleD{40}}{100}}=\dfrac{0.30}{0.40}=\dfrac{3}{4}=0.75
\end{align}$

###The answer


$P\left( \text{male }| \text{ fly} \right)=\dfrac{3}{4}=0.75$

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers surveyed one hundred students on which superpower they would most like to have. The two-way table below displays data for the  sample of students who responded to the survey.

Superpower | Male | Female | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: |
Fly | $30$ | ${10}$ | $40$
Invisibility | $12$ | ${32}$ | $44$
Other | $10$ | ${6}$ | $16$
**TOTAL** | $52$ | $48$ | $100$

**Given that the student chosen was a female, find the probability that the student chose invisibility as their superpower.**

 $P\left( \text{invisibility }| \text{ female} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the student was a female. There were $\purpleD{48}$ total female students in the sample, and $\greenD{32}$ of them chose invisibility.

Superpower | Male | Female | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: |
Fly | ${30}$ | ${10}$ | ${40}$
Invisibility | ${12}$ | $\greenD{32}$ | $44$
Other | ${10}$ | ${6}$ | $16$
**TOTAL** | ${52}$ | $\purpleD{48}$ | $100$

$P\left( \text{invisibility }| \text{ female} \right)=\dfrac{\greenD{32}}{\purpleD{48}}=\dfrac{2}{3}$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{invisibility }| \text{ female} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{invisibility and female} \right)}{P\left( \text{ female} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{{\dfrac{\greenD{32}}{100}}}{\dfrac{\purpleD{48}}{100}}=\dfrac{0.32}{0.48}=\dfrac{2}{3}\end{align}$

###The answer

$P\left( \text{invisibility }| \text{ female} \right)=\dfrac{2}{3}\approx0.667$

assessment_items.question_content:
Sophia made the following two-way table categorizing the US senators in 2015 by their political party and whether or not it was their first term in the senate.

 | Democrat | Republican | Independent | Total
-|-|-|-
First term | $11$ | $28$ | $1$ | $40$
Returning | $33$ | $26$ | $1$ | $60$
**Total** | $44$ | $54$ | $2$ | $100$

[http://www.270towin.com/2015-senate/]: (Source)

**Find the probability that the senator was in the Democratic party, given that the senator was returning to office.**

$P\left( \text{Democrat }| \text{ returning} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the senator was returning to office. There were $\purpleD{60}$ total senators in the sample who were returning to office, and $\greenD{33}$ of them were in the Democratic party.

 | Democrat | Republican | Independent | Total
-|-|-|-
First term | $11$ | $28$ | $1$ | $40$
Returning | $\greenD{33}$ | $26$ | $1$ | $\purpleD{60}$
**Total** | $44$ | $54$ | $2$ | $100$

$P\left( \text{Democrat}| \text{ returning} \right)=\dfrac{\greenD{33}}{\purpleD{60}}=\dfrac{11}{20}=0.55$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{Democrat }| \text{ returning} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{Democrat and returning} \right)}{P\left( \text{returning} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left({\dfrac{\greenD{33}}{100}}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{\purpleD{60}}{100}\right)}=\dfrac{0.33}{0.60}=\dfrac{11}{20}=0.55\end{align}$

###The answer


$P\left( \text{Democrat}| \text{ returning} \right)=\dfrac{11}{20}=0.55$

assessment_items.question_content:
The two-way table below displays demographic data for the  passengers aboard the Titanic.

 | Man | Woman | Child | Total
-|-|-|-|-
First class | $175$ | $144$ | $6$ | $325$
Second class | $168$ | $93$ | $24$ | $285$
Third class | $462$ | $165$ | $79$ | $706$
**Total** | $805$ | $402$ | $109$ | $1316$

**Given that a passenger selected at random was a child, find the probability that the passenger traveled in the third class.**

 $P\left( \text{third class }| \text{ child} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the passenger was a child. There were $\purpleD{109}$ total children in the sample, and $\greenD{79}$ of them traveled in the third class.

 | Man | Woman | Child | Total
-|-|-|-|-
First class | $175$ | $144$ | $6$ | $325$
Second class | $168$ | $93$ | $24$ | $285$
Third class | $462$ | $165$ | $\greenD{79}$ | $706$
**Total** | $805$ | $402$ | $\purpleD{109}$ | $1316$

$P\left( \text{third class }| \text{ child} \right)=\dfrac{\greenD{79}}{\purpleD{109}}$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{third class }| \text{ child} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{child and third class} \right)}{P\left( \text{child} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{{\left(\dfrac{\greenD{79}}{1316}\right)}}{ \left(\dfrac{\purpleD{109}}{1316}\right) }=\dfrac{79}{109}\end{align}$

###The answer:

$P\left( \text{third class }| \text{ child} \right)=\dfrac{79}{109}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The two-way table below gives the thousands of commuters in Massachusetts in 2015 by transportation method and one-way length of commute.


 | Less than $15$ minutes | $15$-$29$ minutes | $30$-$44$ minutes | $45$-$59$ minutes | $60$ or more minutes | Total
-| -| -| -| -| -| -
Private vehicle | $636$ | $908$ | $590$ | $257$ | $256$ | $2647$
Public Transportation | $9$ | $54$ | $96$ | $62$ | $108$ | $329$
Other | $115$ | $70$ | $23$ | $7$ | $7$ | $222$
**Total** | $760$ | $1032$ | $709$ | $326$ | $371$ | $3198$

[//]: (Derived from https://factfinder.census.gov/faces/tableservices/jsf/pages/productview.xhtml?pid=ACS_15_5YR_S0802&prodType=table)

**Given that the commuter used public transportation, find the probability that the commuter had a commute of $60$ or more minutes.**

 $P\left({60+} \text{ minutes }| \text{ public transportation} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the commuter used public transportation. There were $\purpleD{329}$ thousand total commuters in the sample who used public transportation, and $\greenD{108}$ thousand of them had commutes of $60$ or more minutes.

 | Less than $15$ minutes | $15$-$29$ minutes | $30$-$44$ minutes | $45$-$59$ minutes | $60$ or more minutes | Total
-| -| -| -| -| -| -
Private vehicle | $636$ | $908$ | $590$ | $257$ | $256$ | $2647$
Public Transportation | $9$ | $54$ | $96$ | $62$ | $\greenD{108}$ | $\purpleD{329}$
Other | $115$ | $70$ | $23$ | $7$ | $7$ | $222$
**Total**  | $760$ | $1032$ | $709$ | $326$ | $371$ | $3198$

$P\left({60+} \text{ minutes }| \text{ public transportation} \right)=\dfrac{\greenD{108}}{\purpleD{329}}$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left({60+} \text{ minutes }| \text{ public transportation} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left({60+} \text{ and public} \right)}{P\left( \text{public transportation} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\left({\dfrac{\greenD{108}}{3198}}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{\purpleD{329}}{3198}\right)}=\dfrac{108}{329}\end{align}$

###The answer


$P\left({60+} \text{ minutes }| \text{ public transportation} \right)=\dfrac{108}{329}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Shreya made the following table where she categorized countries by their region and by average business startup costs, as a percentage of the per capita gross national income (GNI) for a certain year. She decided that high meant greater than or equal to $8\%$ but less than $70\%$ of the GNI and that very high meant greater than or equal to $70\%$ of the GNI.

Region | Very high | High | Low | Total
-|-|-|-|-
East Asia & Pacific | $3$ | $13$ | $8$ | $24$
Europe & Central Asia | $0$ | $8$ | $17$ | $25$
Latin America & Caribbean | $4$ | $23$ | $5$ | $32$
Middle East & North Africa | $4$ | $9$ | $7$ | $20$
OECD | $0$ | $5$ | $27$ | $32$
South Asia | $0$ | $7$ | $1$ | $8$
Sub-Saharan Africa | $21$ | $22$ | $4$ | $47$
**Total** | $32$ | $87$ | $69$ | $188$

**Find the probability that the country is in the south Asia region, given that Shreya categorized the country's business startup costs as high.**

 $P\left( \text{south Asia }| \text{ high} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that Shreya categorized the country's business startup costs as high. There were $\purpleD{87}$ total countries that she categorized as high, and $\greenD{7}$ of them were in the south Asia region.

Region | Very high | High | Low | Total
-|-|-|-|-
East Asia & Pacific | $3$ | $13$ | $8$ | $24$
Europe & Central Asia | $0$ | $8$ | $17$ | $25$
Latin America & Caribbean | $4$ | $23$ | $5$ | $32$
Middle East & North Africa | $4$ | $9$ | $7$ | $20$
OECD | $0$ | $5$ | $27$ | $32$
South Asia | $0$ | $\greenD{7}$ | $1$ | $8$
Sub-Saharan Africa | $21$ | $22$ | $4$ | $47$
**Total** | $32$ | $\purpleD{87}$ | $69$ | $188$

$P\left( \text{south Asia }| \text{ high} \right)=\dfrac{\greenD{7}}{\purpleD{87}}$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{south Asia }| \text{ high} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{south Asia and high} \right)}{P\left( \text{high} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{{\left(\dfrac{\greenD{7}}{188}\right)}}{ \left(\dfrac{\purpleD{87}}{188}\right) }=\dfrac{7}{87}\end{align}$

###The answer:

$P\left( \text{south Asia }| \text{ high} \right)=\dfrac{7}{87}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Jillian surveyed students at her school and found out the following probabilities:

$\begin{align}
&P(\text{freshman})=0.30
\\\\
&P(\text{plays instrument})=0.20
\\\\
&P(\text{freshman and instrument})=0.10
\end{align}$

**Find the probability that a student plays an instrument given they are a freshman.**

 $P\left( \text{plays instrument }| \text{ freshman} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Use conditional probability formula

$P\left( \text{plays instr. }| \text{ freshman} \right)=\dfrac{P\left( \text{instr. and freshman} \right)}{P\left( \text{ freshman} \right)}$
$\dfrac{P\left( \text{instr. and freshman} \right)}{P\left( \text{ freshman} \right)}=\dfrac{0.10}{0.30}=\dfrac13$

###The answer

$P\left( \text{plays instrument }| \text{ freshman} \right)=\dfrac13$

assessment_items.question_content:
Pedro observed what customers ordered at his ice cream shop and found the following probabilities:

$\begin{align}
&P(\text{vanilla})=0.3
\\\\
&P(\text{sundae})=0.2
\\\\
&P(\text{vanilla and sundae})=0.15
\end{align}$

**Find the probability that a customer ordered vanilla ice cream given they ordered a sundae.**

 $P\left( \text{vanilla }| \text{ sundae} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Use conditional probability formula

$P\left( \text{vanilla }| \text{ sundae} \right)=\dfrac{P\left( \text{vanilla and sundae} \right)}{P\left( \text{sundae} \right)}$
$\dfrac{P\left( \text{vanilla and sundae} \right)}{P\left( \text{sundae} \right)}=\dfrac{0.15}{0.2}=\dfrac{3}{4}$

###The answer

$P\left( \text{vanilla }| \text{ sundae} \right)=\dfrac{3}{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The nursing department of a college surveyed two hundred graduates from their programs about their current work. The college has different levels of degrees in nursing available. The department found the following probabilities:

$\begin{align}
&P(\text{bachelor's degree})=0.45
\\\\
&P(\text{working in nursing})=0.85
\\\\
&P(\text{in nursing and bachelor's})=0.4
\end{align}$

[//]: (Derived from https://bhw.hrsa.gov/sites/default/files/bhw/nchwa/rnsurveyfinal.pdf)

**Find the probability that a graduate is currently working in nursing, given that they earned a bachelor's degree.**

 $P\left( \text{in nursing }| \text{ bachelor's} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Use conditional probability formula

$P\left( \text{in nursing }| \text{ bachelor's} \right)=\dfrac{P\left( \text{in nursing and bachelor's} \right)}{P\left( \text{bachelor's degree} \right)}$
$\dfrac{P\left( \text{in nursing and bachelor's} \right)}{P\left( \text{bachelor's degree} \right)}=\dfrac{0.4}{0.45}=\dfrac{8}{9}$

###The answer

$P\left( \text{in nursing }| \text{ bachelor's} \right)=\dfrac{8}{9}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Vera examined a random sample of deodorants distributed by her company. She found the following probabilities:

$\begin{align}
&P(\text{for men})=0.55
\\\\
&P(\text{botanic scent})=0.18
\\\\
&P(\text{for men and botanic})=0.05
\end{align}$

**Find the probability that a deodorant is for men, given that it has a botanic scent.**

 $P\left( \text{for men }| \text{ botanic} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Use conditional probability formula

$P\left( \text{for men }| \text{ botanic} \right)=\dfrac{P\left( \text{for men and botanic} \right)}{P\left( \text{botanic scent} \right)}$
$\dfrac{P\left( \text{for men and botanic} \right)}{P\left( \text{botanic scent} \right)}=\dfrac{0.05}{0.18}=\dfrac{5}{18}$

###The answer

$P\left( \text{for men }| \text{ botanic} \right)=\dfrac{5}{18}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Denis examined the records of the clients at his gym, and he found these statistics:

$\begin{align}
&P(\text{joined in January})=0.12
\\\\
&P(\text{member for over 6 months})=0.5
\\\\
&P(\text{over 6 months and January})=0.024
\end{align}$

**Find the probability that a client remained a member for more than $6$ months, given that the client joined in January.**

 $P\left( \text{over 6 months }| \text{ January} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Use conditional probability formula

$P\left( \text{over 6 months }| \text{ January} \right)=\dfrac{P\left( \text{over 6 months and January} \right)}{P\left( \text{joined in January} \right)}$
$\dfrac{P\left( \text{over 6 months and January} \right)}{P\left( \text{joined in January} \right)}=\dfrac{0.024}{0.12}=\dfrac{1}{5}=0.2$

###The answer

$P\left( \text{over 6 months }| \text{ January} \right)=\dfrac{1}{5}=0.2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Kiran surveyed a random sample of students in her school district, and she found these statistics:

$\begin{align}
&P(\text{rides bike to school})=0.14
\\\\
&P(\text{has crossing guard})=0.48
\\\\
&P(\text{rides bike and crossing guard})=0.12
\end{align}$

[//]: (Based on https://www.cdc.gov/pcd/issues/2016/15_0573.htm)

**Find the probability that a student rides a bike to school, given that the student's school has a crossing guard.**

 $P\left( \text{rides bike }| \text{ crossing guard} \right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Use conditional probability formula

$P\left( \text{rides bike }| \text{ crossing guard} \right)=\dfrac{P(\text{rides bike and crossing guard})}{P\left( \text{has crossing guard} \right)}$
$\dfrac{P(\text{rides bike and crossing guard})}{P\left( \text{has crossing guard} \right)}=\dfrac{0.12}{0.48}=\dfrac{1}{4}=0.25$

###The answer

$P\left( \text{rides bike }| \text{ crossing guard} \right)=\dfrac{1}{4}=0.25$

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant noted what type of food its customers ordered last week. Here are the results:

![A Venn diagram of probability consists of two overlapping circles within a rectangular box. The circles represent burgers and fries. Probabilities are labeled within as follows. Burger, 30%. Fries, 10%. Burgers and fries, 40%. A probability of 20% is labeled outside of the circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/19c0d5361cafc3131e07ac67d27fcf28ce1d3f63)

**Find the probability that a randomly chosen customer ordered a burger given that they ordered fries. **

$P\left(\text{burger }| \text{ fries}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the customer ordered fries, so we should only look at those customers in the diagram:

![The previous Venn diagram of probability is displayed. All probabilities are removed except fries = 10% and burgers and fries = 40%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3be50941bf88f50e9664540182d997b2303625c4)

So we have $40\%+10\%=50\%$ of customers under the condition "ordered fries", and we can find the probability that a customer in that group ordered a burger:

$P\left(\text{burger }| \text{ fries}\right)=\dfrac{40\%}{50\%}=\dfrac45$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{burger }| \text{ fries} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{burger and fries} \right)}{P\left( \text{fries} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{{40\%}}{40\%+10\%}=\dfrac{40\%}{50\%}=\dfrac45
\end{align}$

###The answer:

$P\left(\text{burger }| \text{ fries}\right)=\dfrac45$

assessment_items.question_content:
A salon owner noted what types of services its clients requested last week. Here are the results:

![A Venn Diagram of probability consists of three circles within a rectangular box. The circles represent dye, haircut, and permanent, with each overlapping the next. Probabilities are labeled within as follows. Dye, 30%. Haircut, 35%. Permanent, 5%. Dye and Haircut, 30%. Haircut and permanent, 10%. A probability of 15% is labeled outside of the circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/316cbf514f64eb8f7175f80a06dd14a41183bf2d)

**Find the probability that a randomly chosen client requested a haircut given they requested a permanent. **

$P\left(\text{haircut }| \text{ permanent}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the client requested a permanent, so we should only look at those clients in the diagram:

![The previous Venn diagram of probability is displayed. All probabilities are removed except haircut and permanent = 10% and permanent = 5%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8adeb92588ac7087f06edc07717d514e0091cd47)

So we have $10\%+5\%=15\%$ of clients under the condition "permanent", and we can find the probability that a client in that group requested a haircut:

$P\left(\text{haircut }| \text{ permanent}\right)=\dfrac{10\%}{15\%}=\dfrac23$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{haircut }| \text{ permanent} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{haircut and permanent} \right)}{P\left( \text{permanent} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{{10\%}}{10\%+5\%}=\dfrac{10\%}{15\%}=\dfrac23
\end{align}$

###The answer:

$P\left(\text{haircut }| \text{ permanent}\right)=\dfrac23$

assessment_items.question_content:
The usher at a wedding asked each of the $80$ guests whether they were a friend of the bride or of the groom. Here are the results:

![A Venn diagram of probability consists of two overlapping circles within a rectangular box. The circles represent bride and groom. Probabilities are labeled within as follows. Bride, 29. Groom, 20. Bride and groom, 30. A probability of 1 is labeled outside of the circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a69243443b5b6c4063e966f186f282dbe3ad2b3f)

**Given that a randomly selected guest is a friend of the groom, find the probability they are a friend of the bride.**

$P\left(\text{bride }| \text{ groom}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the guest is a friend of the groom, so we should only look at those guests in the diagram:

![The previous Venn diagram of probability is displayed. All probabilities are removed except Groom = 20% and bride and groom = 30%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6c02bcfe272d89f6932d77b4289b81c31e666c8d)

So we have $30+20=50$ guests under the condition "groom", and we can find the probability that a guest in that group was a friend of the bride:

$P\left(\text{bride }| \text{ groom}\right)=\dfrac{30}{50}=\dfrac35$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{bride }| \text{ groom} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{bride and groom} \right)}{P\left( \text{groom} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\dfrac{30}{80}}{\dfrac{30+20}{80}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{30}{30+20}=\dfrac{30}{50}=\dfrac35
\end{align}$

###The answer:

$P\left(\text{bride }| \text{ groom}\right)=\dfrac35$

assessment_items.question_content:
A business owner noted the features of the $100$ cars parked at the business. Here are the results:

![Venn diagram of probability consists of three circles within a rectangular box, each overlapping the other two. The circles represent sunroof, 2 doors, and 4 doors. Probabilities are labeled within as follows. Sunroof, 12%. 2 doors, 14%. 4 doors, 25%. 2 doors and sunroof, 18%. 4 doors and sunroof, 20%. 2 doors and 4 doors, 0%. 2 doors, 4 doors, and sunroof, 0%. A probability of 11% is labeled outside of the circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/92a0edfc2e4c8f990610386757ba0f85896ee214)

**Given that a randomly selected car has a sunroof, find the probability the car has 4 doors.**

$P\left(\text{4 doors }| \text{ sunroof}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuitive way

The condition is that the car has a sunroof, so we should only look at those cars in the diagram:

![The previous Venn diagram of probability is displayed. All probabilities are removed except the following. Sunroof, 12%. 2 doors and sunroof, 18%. 4 doors and sunroof, 20%. 2 doors, 4 doors, and sunroof, 0%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a343fe6f372d00c208247f60dcdca0df83dabbd0)

So we have $12+18+0+20=50$ cars under the condition "sunroof", and we can find the probability that a car in that group had 4 doors:

$P\left(\text{4 doors }| \text{ sunroof}\right)=\dfrac{20+0}{50}=\dfrac25$

###Using conditional probability formula

$\begin{align}P\left( \text{4 doors }| \text{ sunroof} \right)&=\dfrac{P\left( \text{4 doors and sunroof} \right)}{P\left( \text{sunroof} \right)} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\dfrac{20}{100}}{\dfrac{12+18+20}{100}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{20}{12+18+20}=\dfrac{20}{50}=\dfrac25
\end{align}$

###The answer:

$P\left(\text{4 doors }| \text{ sunroof}\right)=\dfrac25$
